mission.title: Install and Configure Distribution on Kubernetes
mission.description: The Distribution project provides a toolkit to pack, ship, store, and deliver container content, including a robust implementation of the OCI Distribution Specification for container registries. Installing it allows you to set up a private registry for managing your container images securely and efficiently.

steps.title: Add the Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Helm repository for the Distribution project to your Helm client:
```bash
helm repo add distribution https://charts.distribution.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install the Distribution chart
steps.description:
Install the Distribution Helm chart with the specified version in a new namespace called 'distribution':
```bash
helm install distribution distribution/distribution --version v3.0.0 --namespace distribution --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Distribution pods are running correctly:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace distribution
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Edit the values of the installed chart to set resource limits for the Distribution deployment:
```bash
helm upgrade distribution distribution/distribution --namespace distribution --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi
```

steps.title: Port-forward to access the registry
steps.description:
Set up port forwarding to access the Distribution registry locally:
```bash
kubectl port-forward service/distribution 5000:5000 --namespace distribution
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will result in the Distribution pods running without issues, and you will be able to access the registry at `http://localhost:5000`. You can also verify the resource limits are applied by checking the pod specifications.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add distribution https://charts.distribution.io
helm repo update
helm install distribution distribution/distribution --version v3.0.0 --namespace distribution --create-namespace
helm upgrade distribution distribution/distribution --namespace distribution --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Distribution Helm release from the cluster to remove the deployment and associated resources:
```bash
helm uninstall distribution --namespace distribution
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created during the installation:
```bash
kubectl delete crd distributions.distribution.io
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace distribution
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'distribution' namespace to clean up all remaining resources and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace distribution
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and state of the Distribution deployment:
```bash
helm get values distribution --namespace distribution > distribution-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Distribution chart to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade distribution distribution/distribution --version v3.1.0 --namespace distribution
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Distribution pods are running the new version correctly:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace distribution
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Distribution pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace distribution
``` 
Look for any configuration issues or missing dependencies.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Distribution service, ensure that the service is running and check the port-forwarding command:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace distribution
``` 
If the service is not running, check the pod status and logs.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources allocated
troubleshooting.description:
If the pods are not starting due to resource constraints, verify the resource limits set during installation:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment distribution --namespace distribution
``` 
Adjust the resource limits if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade command fails, check for any errors in the output and ensure that the chart version is compatible:
```bash
helm history distribution --namespace distribution
``` 
You can roll back to the previous version if needed:
```bash
helm rollback distribution <revision-number> --namespace distribution
```

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster and the Helm and kubectl command-line tools installed.
